title: At the NATO Summit, Europe Faces an Accelerating U.S. Decoupling | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Europe arrived at the July 7-8 Ankara NATO summit expecting to showcase rearmament progress, but the Iran war's base-access rift -- Spain's refusal, Italy's objections -- has instead accelerated Pentagon plans to cut U.S. jets, bombers, and troops assigned to NATO, with a formal six-month force-posture review underway. CFR's Liana Fix argues the U.S. will not wait for a negotiated 'NATO 3.0' transition and that Europe must build independent defense-planning and command capacity regardless of the summit's outcome.

insights.claim: European and U.S. media reports indicate the Pentagon may cut the F-16 and F-15 jets assigned to NATO by a third, strategic bombers by half, reduce aerial tankers, and remove a missile-launching submarine and an aircraft carrier from the pool of capabilities assigned to NATO in a crisis.
insights.speaker: Liana Fix
insights.so_what: A concrete, sizable reduction in U.S. hard power committed to European deterrence -- a leading indicator for European defense-industrial replacement demand (jets, bombers, ISR, missile defense).
insights.quote: the United States might reduce the F-16 and F-15 jets assigned to NATO by a third, strategic bombers by half, reduce aerial tankers, and remove a missile-launching submarine and an aircraft carrier
insights.locator: European and U.S. media reports indicate

insights.claim: The Trump administration has already announced the removal of about five thousand U.S. troops from Germany over a six-to-twelve month period and canceled the planned deployment of a long-range fire battalion this year.
insights.speaker: Liana Fix
insights.so_what: A concrete, already-confirmed near-term drawdown that quantifies the pace of decoupling -- a baseline to track against future announcements from the force-posture review.
insights.quote: the removal of about five thousand troops from Germany over a six-to-twelve month period and canceled the planned deployment of a long-range fire battalion this year
insights.locator: Already, the Trump administration has announced

insights.claim: U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth called European allies' refusal of base access during the Iran war "shameful" at the June 18 NATO defense ministers' meeting in Brussels and announced a six-month review of U.S. force posture in Europe.
insights.speaker: Liana Fix
insights.so_what: This review is the formal mechanism that will set the pace and scale of further U.S. withdrawal -- its outcome (due roughly by year-end) is the next hard catalyst to watch.
insights.locator: U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth called the refusal of base access

themes.theme: The Iran war split the alliance

insights.claim: Spain's Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez refused to let U.S. forces operate from jointly operated bases or use Spanish airspace during the Iran war, Italy raised legal objections, and the UK initially hesitated before reaffirming base access -- a split U.S. officials are now citing to justify troop drawdowns.
insights.speaker: Liana Fix
insights.so_what: The base-access rift is the proximate political trigger the Pentagon is using to justify accelerated withdrawal -- watch which allies side with or against Washington on future crisis-response requests as a signal of who bears the next round of drawdown pressure.
insights.locator: Spain’s Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez condemned the war

insights.claim: U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio called the Ankara summit "probably the most important meeting in NATO's history" because there are "some things here that need to be cleared up and fixed," signaling Washington intends to force a reckoning over burden-sharing rather than celebrate Europe's spending gains.
insights.speaker: Liana Fix
insights.so_what: Sets the summit up as confrontational rather than celebratory -- raises the odds of surprise U.S. announcements at Ankara rather than a routine scorecard review.
insights.quote: probably the most important meeting in NATO’s history
insights.locator: U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio calling the summit

insights.claim: European NATO allies have reached the 2 percent of GDP defense-spending target and made real progress toward the new 3.5 percent (plus 1.5 percent on related infrastructure) goal for 2035, aided by the EU's ReArm Europe initiative and SAFE defense loans that give member states greater fiscal flexibility.
insights.speaker: Liana Fix
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of Europe's rearmament fiscal cycle -- relevant for forecasting European defense-sector capex and sovereign debt issuance tied to ReArm Europe/SAFE financing.
insights.locator: Not only have European allies reached their targets

insights.claim: Germany plans to reach 3.5 percent of GDP on defense by 2029 through debt-financed rearmament, Poland is heading toward 5 percent, and the Baltic states are already at or above 3.5 percent -- the clear spending leaders among European allies.
insights.speaker: Liana Fix
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific national defense budgets and issuers likely to see the largest procurement and debt increases -- actionable for European defense-industrial and sovereign-bond positioning.
insights.locator: Germany, Poland, and the Baltic states are leading in spending

insights.claim: NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte said on June 25 in Washington that "Russia is not afraid of commitments, but of capabilities," framing delivered hardware -- not spending totals -- as the metric that matters to the alliance.
insights.speaker: Liana Fix
insights.so_what: A filter for evaluating which European defense programs actually move the needle with Washington: fielded capability, not budget lines, is the relevant KPI.
insights.quote: Russia is not afraid of commitments, but of capabilities.
insights.locator: As NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte said on June 25

insights.claim: Europe's key capability gaps span ISR (satellite systems and early-warning airborne surveillance), air and missile defense, long-range fires, cyber, artificial intelligence, and electronic warfare -- the same domains the U.S. is simultaneously threatening to withdraw assets from.
insights.speaker: Liana Fix
insights.so_what: Maps the specific defense-technology segments -- satellites, missile defense, EW, AI -- where European and allied suppliers face the clearest multi-year demand pull as the U.S. steps back.
insights.locator: These European capability gaps include ISR

insights.claim: U.S. Undersecretary for Defense Elbridge Colby's proposed "NATO 3.0" -- a mutually agreed, structured roadmap for Europe to assume more of the defense burden -- is unlikely to materialize regardless of the Ankara summit's outcome, since the U.S. is moving to withdraw unilaterally rather than wait for Europe to fill the gaps.
insights.speaker: Liana Fix
insights.so_what: Investors and planners should not expect an orderly, negotiated U.S. handoff -- the base case is unilateral and potentially disruptive American capability withdrawal rather than a phased transition.
insights.locator: Europeans had hoped for a mutually agreed process

insights.claim: Despite the U.S. Congress's attempt to put a floor of seventy-six thousand troops under future withdrawals, Fix argues European hopes that this (or fear of duplicating NATO structures) will slow U.S. disengagement are "outdated" -- the U.S. will continue to actively decouple from European security.
insights.speaker: Liana Fix
insights.so_what: Challenges a common European assumption that legislative floors or non-duplication caution will preserve U.S. commitment -- implies Europe should build independent command-and-control capacity regardless of Congressional guardrails.
insights.locator: Despite attempts by the U.S. Congress

themes.theme: Ankara outlook and Europe's path forward

insights.claim: In the best-case Ankara scenario, Europeans strike a friendly tone with President Trump (as at the France-hosted G7 summit) and make progress on Ukraine support, including Kyiv's push for a U.S. license to produce Patriot missiles inside Ukraine; in the worst case, the base-access rift resurfaces and triggers an even faster U.S. withdrawal.
insights.speaker: Liana Fix
insights.so_what: A U.S. license for in-country Patriot production is a concrete near-term catalyst to watch for out of Ankara -- a bullish signal for Ukraine's domestic air-defense-industrial buildout if granted.
insights.locator: In the best case, Europeans are able to strike a friendly tone

insights.claim: Fix concludes Europe must build its own "European way of war" -- designed to deter Russia rather than mirror U.S. force structure -- including independent defense planning and, eventually, its own command-and-control structures, even at the cost of duplicating some NATO functions.
insights.speaker: Liana Fix
insights.so_what: Signals a structural, multi-year buildout of parallel European defense-industrial and command-and-control capacity -- a long-duration theme for European defense contractors and planners independent of U.S. supply chains.
insights.locator: This includes acquiring the key capabilities needed
